content:
# elizaos/elizaos.github.io Monthly Report (March 2026)

## 🚀 Highlights
During March 2026, development focused on maintaining the project's underlying infrastructure and fostering discussions around the integration of AI-driven marketplaces. The primary technical activity involved keeping the framework's web stack current through dependency updates. Meanwhile, community engagement centered on the evolving landscape of AI-to-AI commerce and automated code review workflows, signaling a growing interest in autonomous agent interoperability.

## 🛠️️ Key Developments

### Infrastructure & Dependency Management
The project prioritized stability and security by ensuring the core web framework remains up to date.
*   **Next.js Framework Update:** A significant dependency update was initiated to transition the project from version 16.1.5 to 16.2.1. This update, tracked in [#245](https://github.com/elizaos/elizaos.github.io/pull/245), ensures the documentation and site infrastructure benefit from the latest performance patches and features within the `next` ecosystem.

## 🐛 Issues & Triage

### Active Issues & Future Directions
Discussions this month were dominated by high-level architectural integrations for AI agents, specifically focusing on how agents interact with external services and each other.

*   **AI Code Review Integration:** Ongoing dialogue persists regarding the implementation of automated AI code reviews. Recent contributions to [#4893](https://github.com/elizaos/elizaos.github.io/issues/4893) suggest a move toward utilizing specialized AI marketplaces to handle these tasks.
*   **Agent Payment Solutions:** The community is actively exploring the "AI Agent Payment Implementation Guide" ([#6244](https://github.com/elizaos/elizaos.github.io/issues/6244)). This issue serves as a focal point for discussing how agents can autonomously handle financial transactions, a critical component for the project's mission of decentralization and autonomy.

## 💬 Community & Collaboration
The month saw active participation from community members interested in the intersection of AI agents and decentralized marketplaces. Specifically, there was notable engagement from contributors like `majorelalexis-stack`, who provided input on both code review and payment implementation issues. These interactions highlight a trend toward integrating ElizaOS with external AI-to-AI ecosystems (such as MAXIA), reflecting the project's core philosophy of modularity and open collaboration across the Web3 space.
